narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 53% chance that you might not pass the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold we use to approve admissions. This prediction is based on a careful look at key parts of your profile, like your undergraduate GPA of 2.3, which is below the typical 3.21 average for students in our dataset, your LSAT score of 23 compared to the usual 36.7, and your middle-of-the-pack class rankings in both Year 1 and Year 3—specifically a decile of 5 in each, a bit lower than the averages of 5.71 and 5.53. Your family income in the middle bracket aligns with many applicants, and as a full-time female student of Asian background, you fit patterns seen in about 43.6% and 5.9% of the dataset respectively. The factors that most strongly pushed the model toward predicting a higher risk of bar failure, in order of influence, were your Year 3 class decile, followed closely by your Year 1 decile, your LSAT score, and your family income bracket—all of which nudged the prediction in the unfavorable direction compared to stronger-performing peers. Your undergraduate GPA and racial background had somewhat less but still notable pull in the same concerning way, while your gender as female was the one element that slightly favored a positive outcome, helping a tiny bit toward predicting success. Being full-time, like most applicants at 92.7%, had almost no sway either way. Overall, your profile sits below average on the academic metrics that matter most, putting you at higher risk than students who typically pass the bar on their first attempt. To improve your chances next time, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT after targeted prep to boost that score significantly, as it's a top influencer and highly changeable. Strengthening your academic performance in any ongoing studies could lift those class deciles, directly countering their heavy negative impact. While past GPA and background factors are harder to shift, excelling in law school previews or related courses might help demonstrate growth. Consider resources like LSAT tutoring or bar prep simulations tailored for students starting from your baseline—they've helped many in similar spots turn predictions around. With dedication to these steps, you could realistically flip this outcome in a future application. Keep pushing; your determination as a full-time student is already a strength worth building on.
